schools.summary: Gill marks the remarkableness — Paul addresses the very men who had dragged him from the temple and beaten him (scarcely deserving the name 'men') as 'brethren' (his countrymen) and 'fathers' (the elders), showing how ready he was to put up with affronts and forgive injuries; the meekness that returns courtesy for cruelty (the spirit of Christ, Matthew 5:44), joined to wisdom (conciliating to gain a hearing); the defence an opportunity to preach Christ to his people (the heart longing for their salvation, Romans 9:1-3).

Ellicott: the opening words did their work — one speaking in Hebrew not likely to blaspheme the sacred Hebrew books, the conciliation continued; Clarke: Paul had probably been traduced as a mere Gentile enemy of the Jewish religion, his speaking Hebrew refuting this (showing him a Jew conversant with the sacred tongue); the mob, lately raging, kept the more silence — the wisdom of conciliation (meeting men on their own ground, removing prejudice, becoming all things to all men, 1 Corinthians 9:20), the providence giving Paul a hearing.

themes: he spake in the Hebrew tongue

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: Paul gives such an account as might satisfy the captain he was not the Egyptian and the Jews he was not the enemy to their law and temple they supposed — one of their own nation, of the stock of Israel; Gill: a Jew by birth, a true Hebrew; born in Tarsus, reared in Jerusalem, educated at the feet of Gamaliel (Acts 5:34) in the strict law of the fathers, zealous toward God 'as ye all are this day' (the appeal to common ground, his former zeal matching theirs) — establishing his credentials as a true, learned, zealous Jew, refuting the slander.

themes: brought up at the feet of Gamaliel

schools.summary: Gill: Paul persecuted the Christian religion and its professors (breathing out threatenings and slaughter, haling them from their houses, imprisoning them, consenting to their death), a most bitter, implacable persecutor — showing how great a work of divine power his conversion was (only God could reconcile such a persecutor, 1 Timothy 1:13); the confession establishing the depth of his former enmity (the foil to the grace that converted him, the change the proof of divine power), the appeal to his hearers' shared former zeal, the honesty that owns the past and magnifies grace.

themes: I persecuted this way unto the death

schools.summary: Gill: the high priest (Annas or Caiaphas) could bear witness, Paul perhaps keeping the letter as a testimony; Barnes: 'all the estate of the elders' the whole sanhedrim, Paul receiving letters to the Jewish brethren at Damascus; Paul appeals to the very authorities who commissioned him to persecute (credible witnesses who could not deny his former enmity, corroborating his account); the commission to Damascus (the letters, the journey to bring Christians bound to Jerusalem) the proof of his former zeal — the dramatic contrast (the persecutor on his errand met by Christ), the foil to grace.

themes: the high priest doth bear me witness

schools.summary: Barnes: 'about noon' omitted in Acts 9, mentioned here as remarkable — occurring at midday, showing Paul was not deluded by any meteoric or natural appearance (which occur at night), the light brighter than the noonday sun (Acts 26:13), the supernatural character attested by the time; Ellicott marks the special note of the time; a great light from heaven at noon arresting the persecutor on his errand (the glory of the risen Christ) — the supernatural intervention (no delusion), the sovereign grace that arrested Paul in the midst of his sin, the providence in the timing.

themes: a great light round about me
the sovereign grace that arrested Paul

schools.summary: Robertson: the second aorist 'I fell,' the voice in the genitive (as Acts 9:7), Saul's name repeated each time the account is given (Acts 9:4; 22:7; 26:14) with the same question; Paul falling to the ground (the persecutor prostrate), the voice of the risen Christ 'Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?' — the searching question identifying the persecution of the church with Christ himself (the Head feeling his members' sufferings, the mystical union, Matthew 25:40); the personal, convicting question (the name repeated), the grace that arrests and convicts to save.

themes: Saul, Saul, why persecutest thou me?
the persecution of the church is of Christ

schools.summary: Robertson: 'of Nazareth' (the Nazarene) added here (not in Acts 9:5) because Jesus is named for the first time in the address; Paul's question 'Who art thou, Lord?' (the awakened conscience), Christ's answer 'I am Jesus of Nazareth, whom thou persecutest' — the risen Christ revealing himself as the very Jesus Saul persecuted (the persecutor confronted with the living, risen Lord, his unbelief shattered, the resurrection proved, 1 Corinthians 15:8); 'whom thou persecutest' again identifying Christ with his church; the grace in the revelation that converts.

themes: I am Jesus of Nazareth, whom thou persecutest
the persecutor confronted with the risen Lord

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'they heard not the voice' = they did not hear it as a voice uttering articulate words, for them as though it thundered (John 12:29), reconciling with Acts 9:7 (they heard 'a voice/sound'); Robertson: the accusative here vs the genitive in Acts 9:7 indicating they did not understand what they heard; the companions saw the light and feared but did not hear the articulate words — the harmonization (hearing the sound but not the message, no contradiction), the appearance real (the companions witnesses) but the call personal (the revelation Paul's own).

debates.question: Does Acts 22:9 contradict Acts 9:7 on whether the companions heard the voice?

sides.position: No — in Acts 9:7 they heard the sound; here they did not hear the articulate words (the distinction of hearing the sound and understanding the message)

themes: they saw the light... but heard not the voice
the sound but not the words

schools.summary: Calvin: 'What shall I do, Lord?' the voice of a tamed man, the true turning unto the Lord — when, laying away all fierceness and fury, we bow our necks willingly to be ruled (the mark of genuine conversion, the surrender of the will); Robertson: 'are appointed for thee' (perfect passive of tassō); Paul, lately breathing threatenings, now ready to obey (the submission the fruit of conversion, Acts 9:6); Christ directing him to Damascus to learn what was appointed (the use of human means, Ananias, v.12, the Lord directing through his servants).

themes: the true turning of the tamed persecutor

schools.summary: Gill: the light, above the brightness of the sun, so dazzled his eyes he could not see his way, led by the hand into the city; Ellicott marks the personal recollection (Paul connecting the blindness with its cause, the glory of the light, where Acts 9:8 states only the fact); Paul, who set out armed to ravage the church (v.5), entered blinded, helpless, led by the hand like a child — the humbling of the proud persecutor (the pride brought low, Proverbs 15:33), the contrast between his intent and his arrival, the physical blindness a figure of his spiritual state (the prelude to the inward light).

themes: could not see for the glory of that light
led by the hand into Damascus

schools.summary: Gill: Paul describes Ananias as 'a devout man according to the law' (a strict observer of the law of Moses, moral and ceremonial, of holy life and conversation), this part of his character chosen to commend him to the Jews addressed (showing the man who instructed and baptized Paul was no enemy of the law but a devout, well-reported Jew); the conciliatory description (the gospel not contrary to the law, its agents not enemies of Moses), the wisdom of meeting men where they are (1 Corinthians 9:20); many believing Jews still observed the law.

themes: Ananias, a devout man according to the law
good report of all the Jews
commending the gospel by its agents

schools.summary: Gill: Ananias came to Paul at the house of Judas in the street called Straight, stood by him, and addressed him; Robertson: the same word for 'looked up' and 'receive thy sight' (anablepō); Ananias greeting the former persecutor tenderly as 'Brother Saul' (the grace that embraces the converted persecutor, the love receiving the former enemy, overcoming his earlier fear, Acts 9:13-14) and restoring his sight (the physical sight a figure of the spiritual enlightening, the blindness removed, the conversion completed); the Lord working through his servant (the means appointed, v.10).

themes: the tender reception of the persecutor
restored sight a figure of enlightenment

schools.summary: Gill: 'the God of our fathers hath chosen thee' = from eternity in his purposes, or 'taken thee into his hand' to qualify thee, designing both his call by grace and to apostleship; Paul represents Ananias speaking of 'the God of our fathers' to show the gospel not contrary to the faith of Israel (no new God); Barnes: the ends — to know his will (the plan of salvation), see the Just One (the Messiah, Christ, Acts 3:14, whom he saw on the road), and hear his voice; the divine election and calling of Paul (qualified to be his witness, v.15, the seeing of the risen Christ the ground of his apostleship, 1 Corinthians 15:8).

themes: the God of our fathers hath chosen thee
know his will... see that Just One... hear his voice
grounded in the God of the fathers

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul to be a witness testifying to all men that the Messiah has come, died, risen, and is the Saviour of the world, of what he had seen and heard (the proof of Christ's divine mission); Ellicott: this mission, identical with the Twelve's (Acts 1:8), virtually placed Paul among the apostles; 'unto all men' the universal commission (Jews and Gentiles, the apostle to the Gentiles, v.21); the witness grounded in his own eyewitness experience (the risen Christ seen and heard, 1 Corinthians 15:8) — the apostolic commission to bear universal witness to Christ.

themes: thou shalt be his witness unto all men
of what thou hast seen and heard
witness to all, Jews and Gentiles

schools.summary: Gill presses against delay — many procrastinate obedience to baptism, but no delay ought to be made, it being Christ's command to be complied with at once (the duty of prompt obedience); on 'wash away thy sins,' the careful reading — baptism does not itself wash away sins (against baptismal regeneration), for sins are washed by the blood of Christ (1 John 1:7) received by faith; baptism the sign and seal of the washing, 'calling on the name of the Lord' (faith, the invoking of Christ) the means — the outward sign of the inward washing, not the efficient cause of pardon.

sides.position: Baptism is the sign and seal of the washing away of sins by Christ's blood, received by faith ('calling on the name of the Lord') — not the efficient cause of pardon (against baptismal regeneration)

themes: wash away thy sins, calling on the name of the Lord
baptism the sign of the washing
received by faith, not baptismal regeneration

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul thrusts himself into danger (coming to Jerusalem after the vision), the witness following from the result — Christ said 'they will not receive thy testimony' (v.18) and they did not, establishing both that the Jews were without excuse (persecuting him contrary to all reason, the former persecutor's testimony the more credible) and that Christ was God (prophesying things contrary to expectation, foreknowing the future); Paul receiving the vision in the temple (the place he was charged with profaning, Acts 21:28) while praying — the revelation given in prayer, the occasion of his Gentile commission (v.21).

themes: while I prayed in the temple, I was in a trance
the occasion of the Gentile commission

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul 'saw him' (the Lord Jesus); 'they will not receive' — the inhabitants of Jerusalem, both Jews (hating him as an apostate) and Christians (remembering his persecutions, suspicious of him); Christ directing him to leave at once because his testimony would be rejected; the foreknowledge of Christ (prophesying, contrary to expectation, that they would not receive the former persecutor's testimony, and so it proved — the foreknowledge proving his deity, Chrysostom); the redirection of the mission from the unreceptive Jerusalem to the Gentiles (v.21), the providence ordering the field.

themes: get thee quickly out of Jerusalem
they will not receive thy testimony

schools.summary: Barnes: 'they know' — the Christians know, unlikely to receive their former enemy; beating often done in the synagogue (Matthew 10:17), the converts still meeting with the Jews; Paul protests his former persecution as a reason his testimony should be received (the change in so great a persecutor a striking proof of the truth, the persecutor's witness the more credible by human calculation); yet Christ foreknew they would not receive him (the contrast of human reasoning and divine foreknowledge, the Jews persecuting contrary to all likelihood, without excuse); Paul's longing for his people (Romans 9:1-3), the desire yielding to the command (v.21).

themes: they know that I imprisoned and beat the believers
the plea of his former persecution
the change in the persecutor a proof

schools.summary: Gill: Stephen the proto-martyr, the first to suffer for Christ, stoned to death; Barnes: Paul standing by, consenting to his death, keeping the raiment of those who slew him (Acts 7:58; 8:1) — though not stoning Stephen, with them in spirit (the guilt of the consenting, a partaker in the murder); the depth of Paul's former enmity (his part in the first martyrdom, consenting to the murder of a saint, 'the chief of sinners,' 1 Timothy 1:15), pleaded as proof his conversion should convince (the change in one who consented to the saints' murder a striking proof); 'thy martyr' the penitent's tender recollection.

themes: the blood of thy martyr Stephen
consenting unto his death, kept the raiment
pleaded as proof of his conversion

schools.summary: Barnes: Christ directed Paul to depart (the Christians at Jerusalem would not receive him), 'far hence' — Paul travelling far in the heathen nations, much of his ministry in distant regions; Clarke: this the particular appointment of Paul, the apostle of the Gentiles (Romans 11:13); the commission to the Gentiles defining his apostleship (the gospel to the nations the great theme), the mission Christ's command not his choosing; the redirection from the Jews (his longing, vv.19-20, yielding to the command); the word 'Gentiles' that would provoke the riot (v.22), the Jewish exclusivism inflamed.

themes: Depart: I will send thee far hence unto the Gentiles
the word that would provoke the riot

schools.summary: Gill: the Jews heard Paul patiently until he mentioned his mission to the Gentiles (the rest they bore or thought an idle tale, but a divine mission to the Gentiles they could not bear); at the word 'Gentiles' they erupted, crying 'Away with such a fellow... not fit that he should live' (the fury unleashed, the demand for his death); the Jewish exclusivism inflamed (unable to bear the Gentiles admitted to equal privilege, the pride hating the breaking down of the middle wall, 1 Thessalonians 2:16); the zeal without knowledge (Romans 10:2); the providence overruling the rage for Paul's preservation.

themes: gave him audience unto this word
away with such a fellow from the earth
the riot at the word Gentiles

schools.summary: Barnes: they cast off their outer garments (probably intending to stone Paul, as Acts 7:58) and threw dust into the air (expressive of abhorrence and indignation) — a striking exhibition of rage and vindictive malice; Paul guarded by Roman soldiers, so their only way of expressing wrath was by these tokens (as Shimei against David, 2 Samuel 16:13); the frenzied, impotent fury of the inflamed multitude (unable to reach Paul, the rage spent in gestures), the providence restraining their violence (protecting him through the Romans), the picture of rage without reason.

themes: cast off their clothes, and threw dust

schools.summary: Gill: the captain brought Paul into the castle to save him from the people's rage and examine him privately, though by a wrong and unjustifiable method; Barnes: 'the castle' the tower of Antonia, 'examined' (the word for testing metals by fire) meaning to torture to extract the truth; the captain, ignorant of the case (the speech in Hebrew), ordering Paul scourged to learn why the mob raged — the unjust method (the cruelty of extracting truth by torture, contrary even to Roman law, v.25); the providence overruling (the order the occasion of Paul's asserting his citizenship, v.25, for his protection).

Gill: they bound Paul with thongs to a pillar to be scourged after the Roman manner, and he, before the scourging, asks the centurion whether it was lawful to scourge a Roman, uncondemned; to scourge a Roman citizen was forbidden by law, and to scourge one uncondemned (without trial) a further violation (the double illegality); Paul asserting his citizenship to stay the unjust scourging (the lawful use of his rights, not from cowardice but to prevent an illegal act and protect himself for service, as Acts 16:37); the prudence using the lawful means at the right time, the balance of patient endurance and wise assertion.

themes: is it lawful to scourge a Roman, and uncondemned?
the lawful use of civil rights

schools.summary: Robertson: the better reading 'What art thou about to do?' a sharp warning; Calvin: some marvel the centurion so credulously affirmed it, but their manner was that one claiming Roman citizenship was taken seriously (and if he could not prove it, punished), the claim not lightly made or received; the centurion, alarmed at Paul's claim, hastening to warn the captain ('this man is a Roman') — the gravity of the violation feared (scourging a Roman citizen a grave crime, severely punished, the centurion and captain in danger), the providence using the alarm to stay the scourging and protect Paul.

Ellicott: the pronoun emphatic — 'Thou, the Jew speaking both Greek and Hebrew, art thou a citizen of Rome?' (the combination of so many discordant elements almost incredible); Robertson: 'Thou (emphatic) a Roman? It was unbelievable'; the captain coming himself to ask Paul directly, surprised that a Jew, lately mobbed, seemingly a common man, should be a Roman citizen (the apostle of higher standing than supposed); Paul simply, plainly affirming it ('Yea,' no boasting, the calm dignified claim) — the confirmation leading the captain to fear and release him (v.29).

themes: art thou a Roman? He said, Yea
the surprise at the apostle's standing

schools.summary: Gill: the captain not a Roman born but likely a Grecian or Syrian (by his name Lysias), and as all was venal at Rome the freedom was bought with money, he wondering that so mean a person as Paul should have such a privilege; Barnes: citizenship might be purchased; the captain says he bought his freedom at a great sum, Paul replying 'I was free born' (a citizen by birth, inherited not bought, the higher standing) — the contrast (the freeborn higher than the purchased), the apostle of no mean position; the providence giving Paul the citizenship (born at Tarsus) that would protect him and serve the gospel (his appeals, Acts 25:11).

themes: with a great sum obtained I this freedom

schools.summary: Barnes: they immediately departed, seeing that by scourging him they would have violated the Roman law and exposed themselves to its penalty; the captain also afraid, having bound a Roman citizen (even the binding a violation); the scourging stayed, the soldiers withdrawing, the captain in danger for his act — the injustice prevented by Paul's citizenship (the lawful claim effectual, the high regard for the citizen's rights, the fear of the penalty); the providence using the citizenship to preserve the apostle (the deliverance for further service); the reversal (the captain afraid for having bound the apostle).

themes: they departed from him which should have examined him
the captain afraid... because he had bound him

schools.summary: Gill: Paul kept in the castle all night, the captain wishing to know the certainty of the accusation which he could not yet learn (some saying one thing, some another, the mob, v.24); Barnes: he summoned the sanhedrim, being prevented from scourging Paul, to learn from the Jews the nature of the charge; the captain loosing Paul (the unlawful binding undone), summoning the council, and setting Paul before them — the search for the charge (the lawful inquiry), the providence ordering Paul's appearance before the Sanhedrin (the occasion of the defence, Acts 23, the apostle preserved and brought toward Rome, Acts 23:11).

themes: he would have known the certainty of the accusation
